{"key_event":"Curzon centralization and coercive plague-famine governance","reasoning":"The colonial state was already strong and became somewhat more intrusive through Curzonite centralization, plague powers, sedition law, frontier consolidation, census legibility, and tighter control of municipalities, universities, and information. Civil society gained modestly from press, associations, Congress networks, and transport links, but these gains were offset by famine, plague repression, rural distress, and reduced civic self-government, leaving it only slightly above the prior weak level.","society_change":0.2,"society_power":4.2,"state_change":0.5,"state_power":8.0}
